No_Variant.question_text: What is meant by A a link canal B a perennial canal C an inundation canal?
No_Variant.marking_scheme: Part A: A link canal means a canal that diverts, transfers, or moves water from one river or canal to another, such as linking western rivers to eastern rivers or canals, or between rivers and canals. Award 1 mark for this explanation. Part B: A perennial canal means a canal that supplies water throughout the year, typically supplied from dams or barrages. Award 1 mark for this explanation. Part C: An inundation canal means a canal that supplies water only in the rainy season, taken from rivers when they are high or flooding. Award 1 mark for this explanation.

No_Variant.question_text: Explain the importance of the Indus Water Treaty to Pakistan.
No_Variant.marking_scheme: Award up to 4 marks for explaining the importance. Acceptable points include: It ensures that India does not restrict Pakistan’s water supply, maintaining water supply in Pakistan. It ensures an effective and dependable irrigation system in the Indus Plain. Pakistan has exclusive rights to the waters of the rivers Indus, Jhelum, and Chenab. It maintains agricultural production. It enabled the construction of Tarbela and Mangla dams to store water. It allowed the building of barrages, syphons, and link canals to distribute water. It enabled the construction costs of these works to be shared with Western countries and India. Award marks for developed points, such as how these ensure water availability for agriculture.

No_Variant.question_text: Explain the effects of this deforestation.

Acceptable points include: Soil erosion because there are no roots to hold the soil, leaving it exposed and with no interception. Soil becomes infertile or loses nutrients due to leaching, erosion of the top layer, or no humus formation from leaves. Soil becomes unfit for agriculture. Siltation in reservoirs because exposed soil is washed into rivers, leading to reduced capacity in reservoirs, dams becoming silted up, or reduced hydroelectric power production and irrigation water supply. Flooding because of increased surface runoff and less interception, leading to destruction of buildings, infrastructure, or farmland. Landslides or avalanches because slopes, rocks, or snow are not held by trees, leading to blocked roads or destroyed buildings. Loss of scenery, beauty, or shade leads to a decrease in tourism. Loss of habitat results in species loss, extinctions, animals moving away, or disturbance. Air pollution from burning. Decrease in rainfall due to less transpiration. Fewer trees to take up CO2, affecting global warming or climate change.
